expanded_description: Master molarity as the primary concentration unit in chemistry. Learn to calculate molarity from mass and volume, prepare solutions of specified concentration, and perform dilution calculations using the dilution equation.

core_concept.principle: Molarity Relates Moles of Solute to Liters of Solution
core_concept.explanation: Molarity ($M$) is defined as moles of solute per liter of solution: $M = \dfrac{\text{moles of solute}}{\text{liters of solution}}$. This concentration unit is fundamental to solution chemistry because it directly relates the countable quantity (moles) to a measurable volume, enabling precise preparation of solutions and stoichiometric calculations in solution-phase reactions.

image.alt: Diagram showing molarity formula with moles of solute divided by liters of solution
image.caption: Figure 9.4.1: Molarity ($M$) = moles of solute / liters of solution. Note: volume is total solution volume, not solvent volume.

sections.introduction: Concentration expresses how much solute is present in a given amount of solution. Molarity is the most widely used concentration unit in chemistry because it connects directly to mole-based stoichiometry.

key_concept.definition: Molarity ($M$) is the number of moles of solute dissolved per liter of solution: $M = \dfrac{n_{\text{solute}}}{V_{\text{solution}}}$, where $n$ is in moles and $V$ is in liters. Units: $\text{mol/L}$ or $M$.
key_concept.context: Molarity depends on temperature because solution volume changes with temperature; for temperature-independent concentration, use molality ($m$ = mol/kg solvent).

components.equation: $M = \dfrac{\text{mass of solute} \div \text{molar mass}}{\text{volume of solution in L}}$
components.interpretation: To calculate molarity: (1) convert solute mass to moles using molar mass, (2) convert solution volume to liters, (3) divide moles by liters.

image.alt: Triangle diagram showing M = n/V relationship
image.caption: Figure 9.4.2: The molarity triangle helps remember: $M = n/V$, $n = M \times V$, $V = n/M$.

characteristics: Molarity ($M$): mol solute / L solution (most common in chemistry)
Molality ($m$): mol solute / kg solvent (temperature-independent)
Mass percent: (g solute / g solution) × 100% (industrial use)
Volume percent: (mL solute / mL solution) × 100% (liquid-liquid mixtures)
Parts per million (ppm): mg solute / kg solution (trace analysis)
classifications.behavior: Molarity is preferred for solution stoichiometry because reaction coefficients relate moles, and lab volumes are easily measured in liters.

examples.explanation: Contains 0.1 mol NaCl per liter of solution. Mass of NaCl = $0.1~\text{mol} \times 58.44~\text{g/mol} = 5.844~\text{g}$ dissolved and diluted to 1.00 L total volume.

image.caption: Molarity preparation: dissolve solute, then dilute to mark.

examples.explanation: Mass/volume percent: 5 g glucose per 100 mL solution. Common in biology and medicine; not the same as molarity.

image.caption: Percent concentrations are common in biological applications.

characteristics: Molarity ($M$): depends on solution volume (changes with temperature)
Molality ($m$): depends on solvent mass (temperature-independent)
For dilute aqueous solutions at 25°C: $M \approx m$ (since 1 L water ≈ 1 kg)
Molality used for colligative properties; molarity for stoichiometry
classifications.behavior: Temperature changes affect molarity (volume expands/contracts) but not molality (mass is constant).

examples.explanation: At 50°C, solution volume increases slightly, so molarity decreases to ~0.99 M, but molality remains 1.00 m.

image.caption: Molality is temperature-independent; molarity is not.

characteristics: Weigh solute accurately
Dissolve in less than final volume of solvent
Transfer to volumetric flask
Add solvent to calibration mark (meniscus at line)
Mix thoroughly
classifications.behavior: Never add solute directly to final volume of solvent; dissolution changes volume. Always dilute to the mark after dissolving.

examples.explanation: Moles needed: $0.5000~\text{L} \times 0.200~\text{mol/L} = 0.100~\text{mol}$. Mass: $0.100~\text{mol} \times 74.55~\text{g/mol} = 7.455~\text{g}$. Dissolve 7.455 g KCl in ~400 mL water, transfer to 500 mL volumetric flask, dilute to mark.

image.caption: Volumetric flasks ensure precise solution volumes.

principles.statement: Molarity does not depend on the amount of solution; a 1.0 M solution has the same concentration whether you have 10 mL or 10 L.
principles.application: Taking 25.0 mL from a 0.500 M solution still gives a 0.500 M solution; only the total moles change ($n = M \times V$).
principles.analogy: Like density: a drop of water and a lake both have density 1.00 g/mL; concentration is 'chemical density'.

principles.statement: In molarity, volume refers to the final volume of the solution (solute + solvent), not the volume of solvent used.
principles.application: Dissolving 1 mol NaCl in 1 L water gives >1 L solution, so molarity < 1 M. To make 1 M NaCl, dissolve 1 mol NaCl and dilute to exactly 1 L total volume.
principles.example: This distinction is critical for accurate solution preparation.

sections.section_title: Calculating Molarity from Mass and Volume
sections.introduction: The most common molarity calculation determines concentration from known mass of solute and final solution volume. This skill is essential for preparing standard solutions.

key_concept.definition: To calculate molarity: $M = \dfrac{\text{mass (g)} \div \text{molar mass (g/mol)}}{\text{volume (L)}}$. Always convert mass to moles first, and volume to liters.
key_concept.context: This calculation assumes the solute is pure and the volume is the final solution volume after dissolution.

process_flow.description: Note the mass of solute (with units), molar mass (or calculate from formula), and solution volume (with units).
Use molar mass: $n = \dfrac{\text{mass}}{\text{molar mass}}$. Ensure units cancel: $\text{g} \div (\text{g/mol}) = \text{mol}$.
If volume is in mL, divide by 1000: $V(\text{L}) = V(\text{mL}) \div 1000$. Molarity requires liters.
Divide moles by liters: $M = n \div V$. Report with correct units ($\text{mol/L}$ or $M$) and significant figures.
process_flow.result: Molarity with proper units and sig figs

worked_examples.title: Basic Molarity Calculation
worked_examples.problem: Calculate the molarity of a solution prepared by dissolving 29.22 g of NaCl in water and diluting to 500.0 mL.

step_by_step.explanation: Mass NaCl = 29.22 g; Molar mass NaCl = 58.44 g/mol; Volume = 500.0 mL.

worked_examples.final_answer: The molarity is $1.000~\text{M}$ NaCl. Four significant figures are retained because all given values have four sig figs.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Basic Molarity Calculation from Mass and Volume

worked_examples.title: Molarity with Molar Mass Calculation
worked_examples.problem: What is the molarity of a solution containing 15.0 g of glucose ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$) in 250.0 mL of solution?
worked_examples.final_answer: The molarity is $0.333~\text{M}$ glucose. Three significant figures match the precision of the given mass (15.0 g).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Molarity Calculation with Molar Mass Determination

worked_examples.title: Finding Mass from Molarity and Volume
worked_examples.problem: How many grams of $\text{KNO}_3$ are needed to prepare 2.00 L of 0.150 M $\text{KNO}_3$ solution?

step_by_step.explanation: From $M = n/V$, solve for moles: $n = M \times V$.

worked_examples.final_answer: $30.3~\text{g}$ of $\text{KNO}_3$ are needed. This calculation is the reverse of molarity determination and is used when preparing solutions of specified concentration.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Calculating Solute Mass from Desired Molarity

worked_examples.title: Molarity with Density Conversion
worked_examples.problem: Concentrated sulfuric acid is 18.0 M $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ and has a density of 1.84 g/mL. What mass of $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ is in 1.00 L of this solution?

step_by_step.step: Calculate Moles of H₂SO₄ in 1.00 L

step_by_step.explanation: Mass of 1.00 L solution = $1000~\text{mL} \times 1.84~\text{g/mL} = 1840~\text{g}$. Mass percent = $(1766/1840) \times 100\% = 96.0\%$.

worked_examples.final_answer: 1.00 L of 18.0 M $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ contains $1.77 \times 10^3~\text{g}$ of $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$. The density allows conversion between volume and mass of solution for additional calculations like mass percent.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Molarity with Density for Mass Calculations

worked_examples.title: Molarity of Ionic Compounds: Ion Concentrations
worked_examples.problem: What are the concentrations of all ions in a 0.200 M solution of $\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3$?

step_by_step.explanation: For every 1 mol $\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3$, get 2 mol $\text{Al}^{3+}$ and 3 mol $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$. Multiply compound molarity by ion coefficients.

worked_examples.final_answer: In 0.200 M $\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3$: $[\text{Al}^{3+}] = 0.400~\text{M}$ and $[\text{SO}_4^{2-}] = 0.600~\text{M}$. The total ion concentration is $0.400 + 0.600 = 1.00~\text{M}$. This calculation is essential for predicting colligative properties and reaction stoichiometry in solution.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Ion Concentrations from Ionic Compound Molarity

sections.introduction: Dilution is the process of reducing solution concentration by adding more solvent. The dilution equation relates initial and final concentrations and volumes, enabling precise preparation of dilute solutions from concentrated stock.

key_concept.definition: The dilution equation: $M_1V_1 = M_2V_2$, where $M_1$ and $V_1$ are the initial (concentrated) molarity and volume, and $M_2$ and $V_2$ are the final (diluted) molarity and volume. This equation works because moles of solute are conserved during dilution: $n_1 = n_2$.
key_concept.context: Volumes $V_1$ and $V_2$ must be in the same units (both mL or both L); molarities must both be in M. The equation assumes volumes are additive, which is approximate but valid for dilute aqueous solutions.

categories.description: Laboratories often prepare working solutions by diluting concentrated stock solutions. The dilution equation calculates the volume of stock needed.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Rearrange $M_1V_1 = M_2V_2$ to solve for $V_1 = \dfrac{M_2V_2}{M_1}$. Measure $V_1$ of stock, transfer to volumetric flask, dilute to $V_2$ with solvent.

examples.process: Preparing 500.0 mL of 0.100 M HCl from 12.0 M stock
examples.explanation: $V_1 = \dfrac{0.100~\text{M} \times 500.0~\text{mL}}{12.0~\text{M}} = 4.17~\text{mL}$. Measure 4.17 mL of 12.0 M HCl, dilute to 500.0 mL.

image.caption: Always add acid to water, not water to acid, for safety.

examples.explanation: Repeated dilutions (e.g., 1:10 each step) create a concentration series for calibration curves or sensitivity testing.

image.caption: Serial dilutions create logarithmic concentration series.

categories.description: When a solution is diluted by adding solvent, the dilution equation calculates the new, lower concentration.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Solve $M_1V_1 = M_2V_2$ for $M_2 = \dfrac{M_1V_1}{V_2}$. $V_2$ is the final total volume after dilution.

examples.process: Diluting 25.0 mL of 0.500 M NaOH to 200.0 mL

image.caption: Dilution reduces concentration proportionally to volume increase.

categories.description: When two solutions of the same solute but different concentrations are mixed, total moles are conserved: $M_1V_1 + M_2V_2 = M_{\text{final}}V_{\text{final}}$.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Calculate total moles from each solution, sum them, divide by total volume to get final molarity.

examples.process: Mixing 100.0 mL of 0.200 M NaCl with 150.0 mL of 0.100 M NaCl

image.caption: Mixing solutions: total moles conserved, volume additive.

principles.statement: Adding solvent changes volume and concentration but not the number of moles of solute: $n_{\text{initial}} = n_{\text{final}}$, hence $M_1V_1 = M_2V_2$.
principles.application: Diluting 10.0 mL of 1.00 M solution to 100.0 mL gives 0.100 M: moles remain $0.0100~\text{mol}$, volume increases 10×, concentration decreases 10×.
principles.analogy: Like adding water to juice: the amount of juice concentrate (moles) stays the same, but the drink (solution) becomes less concentrated.

principles.statement: The dilution equation assumes $V_{\text{final}} = V_{\text{initial}} + V_{\text{added solvent}}$. This is valid for dilute aqueous solutions but not exact for concentrated solutions or non-aqueous solvents.
principles.application: For precise work with concentrated solutions, measure final volume in a volumetric flask rather than assuming additivity.
principles.example: This approximation is why volumetric flasks are used: they measure final solution volume directly.

sections.introduction: Apply molarity and dilution concepts to realistic chemistry problems. These examples demonstrate calculations encountered in laboratory and industrial settings.

worked_examples.title: Preparing a Standard Solution
worked_examples.problem: Describe how to prepare 250.0 mL of 0.100 M $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$ solution from solid $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$.

step_by_step.explanation: Weigh 2.650 g $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$, dissolve in ~200 mL distilled water in beaker, transfer quantitatively to 250.0 mL volumetric flask, rinse beaker into flask, dilute to mark with water, mix thoroughly.
step_by_step.calculation: Procedure: weigh → dissolve → transfer → dilute → mix

worked_examples.final_answer: Weigh 2.650 g of $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$, dissolve in distilled water, transfer to a 250.0 mL volumetric flask, dilute to the calibration mark with water, and mix thoroughly. The resulting solution is 0.100 M $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$. Quantitative transfer (rinsing) ensures all solute reaches the flask.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Preparing Standard Solution from Solid Solute

worked_examples.title: Dilution of Concentrated Acid
worked_examples.problem: Concentrated nitric acid is 15.8 M $\text{HNO}_3$. What volume of this stock is needed to prepare 1.00 L of 0.500 M $\text{HNO}_3$? Describe the safe procedure.

step_by_step.explanation: ALWAYS add acid to water, never water to acid (prevents violent boiling). Measure ~900 mL distilled water in 1 L volumetric flask. Slowly add 31.6 mL concentrated $\text{HNO}_3$ with stirring. Dilute to mark with water, mix.
step_by_step.calculation: Safety: Add acid to water, not water to acid

worked_examples.final_answer: 31.6 mL of 15.8 M $\text{HNO}_3$ is needed. Safe procedure: add the concentrated acid slowly to ~900 mL of water in a volumetric flask, then dilute to 1.00 L. Adding acid to water dissipates heat safely; adding water to concentrated acid can cause violent boiling and splattering.

worked_examples.title: Ion Concentrations in Mixed Solutions
worked_examples.problem: What are the concentrations of all ions after mixing 100.0 mL of 0.200 M $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$ with 150.0 mL of 0.100 M $\text{Ba}(\text{NO}_3)_2$? (Note: $\text{BaSO}_4$ precipitates.)

step_by_step.step: Calculate Initial Moles of Each Ion
step_by_step.calculation: Na⁺: 0.0400 mol; SO₄²⁻: 0.0200 mol; Ba²⁺: 0.0150 mol; NO₃⁻: 0.0300 mol

step_by_step.explanation: After precipitation: $\text{Na}^+$: 0.0400 mol (spectator); $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$: $0.0200 - 0.0150 = 0.0050~\text{mol}$; $\text{Ba}^{2+}$: 0 mol (all precipitated); $\text{NO}_3^-$: 0.0300 mol (spectator).
step_by_step.calculation: Remaining: Na⁺ 0.0400 mol, SO₄²⁻ 0.0050 mol, NO₃⁻ 0.0300 mol

worked_examples.final_answer: After mixing and precipitation: $[\text{Na}^+] = 0.160~\text{M}$, $[\text{SO}_4^{2-}] = 0.020~\text{M}$, $[\text{NO}_3^-] = 0.120~\text{M}$, $[\text{Ba}^{2+}] \approx 0$. This problem combines molarity, dilution, and precipitation stoichiometry—common in analytical chemistry.

worked_examples.title: Serial Dilution for Calibration
worked_examples.problem: A stock solution is 1.00 × 10⁻² M. Describe how to prepare 100.0 mL each of 1.00 × 10⁻³ M, 1.00 × 10⁻⁴ M, and 1.00 × 10⁻⁵ M solutions by serial dilution.

step_by_step.explanation: Each step: dilute 10.0 mL of previous solution to 100.0 mL (1:10 dilution). This reduces concentration by factor of 10 each step.
Pipet 10.0 mL of 1.00 × 10⁻² M stock into 100.0 mL volumetric flask, dilute to mark. $M_2 = (1.00 \times 10^{-2}~\text{M} \times 10.0~\text{mL}) / 100.0~\text{mL} = 1.00 \times 10^{-3}~\text{M}$.
Pipet 10.0 mL of 1.00 × 10⁻³ M solution into 100.0 mL volumetric flask, dilute to mark. $M = 1.00 \times 10^{-4}~\text{M}$.
Pipet 10.0 mL of 1.00 × 10⁻⁴ M solution into 100.0 mL volumetric flask, dilute to mark. $M = 1.00 \times 10^{-5}~\text{M}$.

worked_examples.final_answer: Serial 1:10 dilutions: (1) 10.0 mL stock → 100.0 mL = 1.00 × 10⁻³ M; (2) 10.0 mL of (1) → 100.0 mL = 1.00 × 10⁻⁴ M; (3) 10.0 mL of (2) → 100.0 mL = 1.00 × 10⁻⁵ M. Serial dilutions are efficient for preparing concentration series for calibration curves or sensitivity studies.

worked_examples.title: Molarity in Reaction Stoichiometry
worked_examples.problem: What volume of 0.250 M $\text{HCl}$ is required to react completely with 0.500 g of $\text{CaCO}_3$? $\text{CaCO}_3(s) + 2\text{HCl}(aq) \rightarrow \text{CaCl}_2(aq) + \text{CO}_2(g) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$

step_by_step.step: Use Mole Ratio to Find Moles of HCl
step_by_step.explanation: From equation: 1 mol $\text{CaCO}_3$ reacts with 2 mol $\text{HCl}$. $n_{\text{HCl}} = 0.004995~\text{mol} \times 2 = 0.00999~\text{mol}$.

worked_examples.final_answer: 40.0 mL of 0.250 M $\text{HCl}$ is required. This calculation combines mass-to-mole conversion, stoichiometric mole ratio, and molarity-to-volume conversion—the core workflow for solution stoichiometry.

sections.introduction: Avoid these frequent errors when working with molarity and dilutions; apply expert strategies for accurate solution preparation and calculations.

key_concept.definition: Common molarity errors include: confusing solution volume with solvent volume, forgetting to convert mL to L, misapplying the dilution equation, and mishandling significant figures in multi-step calculations.
key_concept.context: Careful attention to units, volume definitions, and calculation sequence prevents errors that compromise solution accuracy.

classifications.type: Mistake: Using Solvent Volume Instead of Solution Volume
characteristics: Adding solute to 1 L solvent and calling it 1 L solution
Ignoring volume change upon dissolution
Getting molarity higher than intended
Inaccurate solution preparation
classifications.behavior: Dissolving solute changes total volume; molarity requires final solution volume, not initial solvent volume.

examples.explanation: Wrong: Add 58.44 g NaCl to 1 L water. Correct: Dissolve 58.44 g NaCl in water, then dilute to exactly 1 L total volume in volumetric flask.

image.caption: Volumetric flasks measure solution volume; beakers do not.

characteristics: Using mL directly in $M = n/V$ without converting to L
Getting molarity 1000× too large
Dimensional analysis fails
Answers off by orders of magnitude
classifications.behavior: Molarity is mol/L; using mL gives mol/mL, which is 1000× larger than M. Always convert volume to liters.

image.caption: Always convert mL to L for molarity calculations.

characteristics: Mixing mL and L for V₁ and V₂
Solving for wrong variable
Forgetting that moles are conserved
Getting impossible concentrations (e.g., diluted solution more concentrated)
classifications.behavior: The dilution equation is simple but requires consistent units and correct algebraic rearrangement.

examples.process: Diluting 10 mL of 1 M to 100 mL

image.caption: M₁V₁ = M₂V₂: initial moles = final moles.

classifications.type: Mistake: Significant Figure Errors in Multi-Step
classifications.value: Carry Extra Digits, Round Only Final Answer
characteristics: Rounding intermediate mole values too early
Losing precision in final molarity
Inconsistent sig figs across calculation steps
Final answer precision doesn't match given data
classifications.behavior: Intermediate rounding accumulates error; keep extra digits until final step, then round to match least precise given value.

examples.explanation: Given: 2.5 g (2 sig figs), 120.0 mL (4 sig figs). Moles: 2.5/58.44 = 0.04278 (keep extra); M = 0.04278/0.1200 = 0.3565 → round to 0.36 M (2 sig figs).

image.caption: Round only the final answer to match given data precision.

classifications.type: Pro Tip: Use the Molarity Calculation Checklist
characteristics: Mass converted to moles? Check.
Volume converted to liters? Check.
Units cancel to give mol/L? Check.
Significant figures match given data? Check.
Answer magnitude reasonable? Check.
classifications.behavior: This systematic check catches unit errors, calculation mistakes, and unreasonable answers before submitting.

examples.explanation: Given: 4.00 g NaOH, 500.0 mL. Moles: 4.00/40.00 = 0.100 mol; Volume: 0.5000 L; M = 0.100/0.5000 = 0.200 M. Check: units mol/L ✓, sig figs 3 ✓, magnitude reasonable ✓.

classifications.value: Prevent Lab Errors with Proper Labeling
characteristics: Include compound name, molarity, date, preparer
Note if solution is light-sensitive or unstable
Use permanent labels on glassware
Double-check calculations before labeling
classifications.behavior: Clear labeling prevents using wrong concentration in experiments, which can invalidate results or cause safety issues.

examples.explanation: Label: '0.100 M Na₂CO₃, prepared 15-Mar-2024, J. Smith'. Include hazard info if applicable. Verify calculation before labeling.

principles.statement: For concentration problems: (1) Given mass and volume? Calculate molarity directly. (2) Given initial and final concentrations/volumes? Use $M_1V_1 = M_2V_2$. (3) Given molarity and volume, need mass? Use $n = MV$, then mass = $n \times M_{\text{molar}}$.
principles.application: Problem: 'Prepare 250 mL of 0.1 M NaCl' → need mass: $n = 0.1 \times 0.250 = 0.025~\text{mol}$; mass = $0.025 \times 58.44 = 1.46~\text{g}$. Problem: 'Dilute 10 mL of 1 M to 100 mL' → use $M_1V_1 = M_2V_2$.
principles.analogy: Like choosing a tool: hammer for nails, screwdriver for screws. Choose the right equation for the given information.

principles.principle: Molarity Connects Macroscopic and Molecular Scales
principles.statement: Molarity bridges measurable lab quantities (grams, mL) to molecular quantities (moles, particles), enabling quantitative prediction of reaction outcomes in solution.
principles.application: Knowing a solution is 0.100 M $\text{HCl}$ tells you it contains $6.022 \times 10^{22}$ $\text{H}^+$ ions per liter, enabling prediction of how much base it can neutralize.
principles.example: This bridging function is why molarity is the cornerstone of solution stoichiometry and analytical chemistry.

summary: Molarity ($M$) is moles of solute per liter of solution: $M = n/V$. It is the primary concentration unit in chemistry because it directly relates moles (for stoichiometry) to measurable volume. To calculate molarity: convert solute mass to moles using molar mass, convert solution volume to liters, divide moles by liters. To prepare a molar solution: weigh solute, dissolve in less than final volume of solvent, transfer to volumetric flask, dilute to calibration mark, mix. The dilution equation $M_1V_1 = M_2V_2$ relates initial and final concentrations and volumes; it works because moles of solute are conserved during dilution. Critical skills include: converting units correctly (mL to L), using total solution volume (not solvent volume), applying the dilution equation with consistent units, and maintaining proper significant figures. Common mistakes include using solvent volume instead of solution volume, forgetting mL-to-L conversion, misapplying the dilution equation, and rounding intermediate values too early. Molarity enables solution stoichiometry: $n = M \times V$ converts between volume and moles for reaction calculations. Mastery of molarity and dilution is foundational for laboratory work, analytical chemistry, and all solution-phase reactions.
